segments.text: idea is can we find a rule that underlies a pattern in data?
And here are some examples of this.
The basic idea is if we have examples,
which are like if I put a cylinder and a square,
cylinder and a cube on this pink block, I get a noise.
If I put just a cylinder on the pink block,
I don't get a noise.
And you want to discover underlying rules based on the data
that you observed.
And so why would you want to do this?
There's a couple reasons why you would want to do this.
The first reason why you would like to do this
is because you might want something
that you can explain to humans.
You can explain this underlying pattern exists in this data.
It explains why the data appears
as it does appear and then humans can go in and analyze it
or something like that.
So recently, there's been a big focus
on like using large language models
for scientific inquiry and other things like that
by coming up with good explanations
for why data is the way it is.
So if we were able to do that, that would be really interesting.
Another thing is language models are not particularly
good at coming up with they're not particularly
good at being consistent about difficult tasks
across very large numbers of examples.
So if you could look at all of the data at once
in for general rules from them, put those rules in a prompt,
and then apply that prompt to make predictions
on new examples, you might be able to raise your overall
accuracy as well.
So that's how humans learn as well.
We don't just memorize each example.
If we just look at a few examples,
then we might not generalize well to new examples.
So we kind of tried to abstract away general rules.
So this is also similar to program induction
from input output examples, which I talked
during the code generation class.
So you have input output examples.
And from them, you would like to come up with general rules.
But this is a little bit more general.
It doesn't necessarily need to be a program that you're
inducing.
It could be a grammar or it could be an explanation
or it could be anything else like this.
So there's a bit of work on rule induction with LLMs.
It's pretty recent work.
But I think it's pretty interesting.
So the first one is hypothesis generation.
Or the first step of this particular work here
is hypothesis generation.
And basically what it does is it takes all of these input
output examples.
And from these input output examples,
it predicts these rules.
Like the answer is always one.
Or you want to pick the smallest one
where you want to pick the first element.
And then you evaluate it.
And so you pick the smallest one.
And you can either evaluate it using another language model
or you can evaluate it using a symbolic evaluator.
If it's a program, you could use a symbolic evaluator.
If it's a language model, you could just
ask the language model to pick answer one always
or pick the smallest one or pick the first element.
And then you get lots of outputs.
And then when you get lots of outputs,
you then can compare them against the expected outputs
and verify whether the rule is correct.
Verify whether the rule gives you the appropriate answer.
And once you've done that, you can go back
and do hypothesis refinement and maybe even give this feedback
about what was wrong and gradually
refine more accurate and more complex hypotheses.
This is another variant of this idea,
which uses different methodology.
I think both are completely valid.
But this one has a little bit higher data constraints.
So basically, what we do is we use hypotheses
in chain of thought reasoning and keep ones that
give resulting correct answers.
So this is the step where they're trying to induce rules.

it has three panes.

the language model would just solve the problem.

The digit is one.

is also correct.
And then they extract particular rules, like six plus four
equals 11 and seven plus one plus one equals 10.
And they add this to the rule library.
So then the question is, how do they extract the rules?
The way they extract the rules is they have an in context prompt,
which surrounds the rules by basically XML tags.
This is a rule that should be extracted.
And so then anything that is in an XML tag,
they, when you get the correct answer,
they extract and add that to the rule library.
And then conversely, like if the derivation,
if the answer is wrong, they just don't add it.
Or they added as a negative example and say,
this is an incorrect rule.
And then in the final step where they do deductive reasoning,
they can then go ahead and use these rules and improve accuracy.
And they demonstrate that that helps.
So basically, these are two different approaches.
One is extracting directly from the chain of thought.
The other is a priori trying to generate rules
from the whole rule base and then verifying them.
Notably, both of these require verifiers.
And so in some recent work, which I hope will be an archive
very soon, we took a look at whether language models
themselves can verify their own hypotheses.
And so that removes the symbolic verifier here
by just asking the language model,
whether the output is correct or not.
And we found that with very powerful language models,
like GPD4, you can actually do that as well.
So that removes the necessity if you have a symbolic
verifier in the loop as well.

and like use these to improve language model-based systems.
